does: ValueCompass introduces a systematic framework for identifying and measuring value alignment between humans and large language models. The framework is grounded in psychological theory and informed by a comprehensive systematic review, establishing a set of fundamental human values that can be operationalized across different contexts. The researchers apply this framework to evaluate alignment across four real-world scenarios—collaborative writing, education, public sectors, and healthcare—revealing empirical misalignments between human values and LLM outputs. Notably, they find that humans frequently prioritize values like National Security that LLMs largely reject, and demonstrate that value priorities vary significantly across domains, suggesting context-dependent rather than universal alignment targets.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research concerned with value alignment in AI systems, extending earlier philosophical and technical work on AI safety and alignment beyond narrow metrics. It draws on psychological theories of human values and connects to recent empirical studies examining whether language models encode or reflect human preferences and ethical judgments. The research responds to limitations in prior alignment frameworks that often treat values as static and universal, addressing instead the recognition within social science and ethics that values are contextual and diverse. It complements concurrent work on measuring and auditing LLM behavior across different populations and scenarios, bringing together psychological validity with AI evaluation methodology.
enables: This framework establishes infrastructure for developing context-aware alignment strategies that can adapt AI system outputs to reflect situation-specific human values rather than imposing uniform value targets. The identification of specific misalignments points toward targeted interventions for particular domains—enabling researchers to build guardrails or training approaches tailored to healthcare, education, or government contexts. By demonstrating that values diverge across scenarios, the work opens directions for multi-objective alignment methods and value-driven fine-tuning approaches. It also provides foundation for auditing AI systems' alignment with societal values, enabling policy frameworks and accountability mechanisms that evaluate AI responsibly across diverse stakeholder groups and application domains.
